coredata.dc:title: Tandem CQDs loaded triple metal oxide interface-reinforced built-in electric field for a wide-spectral-responsive photocatalyst
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects

coredata.dc:description: Focusing at efficient Cefixime (CEFX) removal, a delicate ultrathin-Bi2WO6 (UBWO) stacked quasi-CoFe2O4 binanosheets (BT) were meticulously designed and integrated with spherical MnWO4 (MWO) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs), forming a n-p-n CQDs@MWO/CFO:UBWO nanohybrid. XRD, XPS, TEM and other systematic analysis demonstrated the vertically aligned layer by layer assembly of UBWO:CFO BT, and a shift in the highly exposed (211) facet of UBWO after nanohybrid formation as well as large surface area (323.33 m2/g) was achieved. UV–vis DRS, PL and EIS proved high separation efficiency, prolonged life time and photogeneration of more charge carriers with absorption of full light spectrum. As expected, the optimized 5 %CQDs@0.25MWO/0.1CFO:UBWO nanohybrid displayed excellent visible light absorption and outstanding CEFX photodegradation whose kinetic constant were 21.11, 12.5, 8.82 folds higher than UBWO, CFO and MWO counterparts. This enhanced photodegradation performance ascribed to the dual strategy as follows: (i) Cascaded charge transfer with the Built-in electric field (BIEF) after n-p-n heterojunction, (ii) the upconverted PL and the electron reservoir properties of CQDs. Degradation efficiency maintained over six consecutive cycles affirming the superior recyclability of the photocatalyst. A possible degradation pathway was proposed based on GC-MS/MS analysis and the end products toxicity were systematically predicted using ECOSAR program. The toxicity of the end product was investigated against bacterial species. The proposed work provides a novel way for the fabrication of efficient photocatalyst with excellent practical applicability.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Focusing at efficient Cefixime (CEFX) removal, a delicate ultrathin-Bi2WO6 (UBWO) stacked quasi-CoFe2O4 binanosheets (BT) were meticulously designed and integrated with spherical MnWO4 (MWO) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs), forming a n-p-n CQDs@MWO/CFO:UBWO nanohybrid. XRD, XPS, TEM and other systematic analysis demonstrated the vertically aligned layer by layer assembly of UBWO:CFO BT, and a shift in the highly exposed (211) facet of UBWO after nanohybrid formation as well as large surface area (323.33 m2/g) was achieved. UV–vis DRS, PL and EIS proved high separation efficiency, prolonged life time and photogeneration of more charge carriers with absorption of full light spectrum. As expected, the optimized 5 %CQDs@0.25MWO/0.1CFO:UBWO nanohybrid displayed excellent visible light absorption and outstanding CEFX photodegradation whose kinetic constant were 21.11, 12.5, 8.82 folds higher than UBWO, CFO and MWO counterparts. This enhanced photodegradation performance ascribed to the dual strategy as follows: (i) Cascaded charge transfer with the Built-in electric field (BIEF) after n-p-n heterojunction, (ii) the upconverted PL and the electron reservoir properties of CQDs. Degradation efficiency maintained over six consecutive cycles affirming the superior recyclability of the photocatalyst. A possible degradation pathway was proposed based on GC-MS/MS analysis and the end products toxicity were systematically predicted using ECOSAR program. The toxicity of the end product was investigated against bacterial species. The proposed work provides a novel way for the fabrication of efficient photocatalyst with excellent practical applicability.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Since the discovery of antibiotics in the 1920s, the consumption of antibiotics has elevated worldwide in health care sector as therapeutic and veterinary drugs [1]. Usually, these antibiotics are highly active at very minimal concentrations, and are absorbed into the blood streams which persists in the body for complete therapeutic effects [2]. The various sources of contamination of these antibiotics include hospital effluents, improper drug disposal, municipal waste water and the pharmaceutical industry [3–5]. Particularly, the poor sanitation structures in the developing areas where the human waste containing this pharmaceutical component are washed off from ground into the fresh water [5].

Cefixime (CEFX), the most active third generation cephalosporins acts against wide range of gram-negative and gram-positive bacteria. Their existence even at minimal concentrations in water resources can substantially increase bacterial resistance leading to development of antibiotic resistance in microorganism [6]. Particularly, the high contents of CEFX at a concentration range from 278 to 422 ng/L are discovered in aquatic environments [7]. In accordance with the statistical report from WHO, annually antibiotic resistance kills 700,000 people worldwide and considered as one of the three major threats by world health organization (WHO) [8]. Hence, it is crucial to develop an efficient method for the treatment of pharmaceutical pollutants from water bodies.

Among the various advanced oxidation processes (AOPs), semiconductor based photocatalytic technology are considered as efficient method where it utilizes the light to degrade the toxic pollutants [9–12]. In literature, the most widely employed semiconductor oxide materials are TiO2 and ZnO [13–18]. However, as a single material, the tendency of recombination of the charge carriers is much higher which can subsequently hinder the photocatalytic performance. One of the effective strategies to overcome this issue is interface of two semiconductors with suitable band alignment for improved spatial separation of the charge carrier to promote the reduction/oxidation reaction between the organic contaminant and charge carriers[19–21].

Bismuth tungstate (BWO), an n-type semiconductor possesses a unique layered structure consisting of [Bi2O2]2 and [WO6]2- layers. This unique layered structure offers a predominant large surface area to absorb H2O molecules, thereby improving the catalytic surface-active sites for reaction to take place [22]. Particularly, the charge carrier life time of ultrathin BWO nanosheets (83.2 nanoseconds (ns)) is much longer than the bulk BWO (14.7 ns) [23–25]. The nanosheet structure of BWO were attracted widely among the series of different morphologies of BWO such as microspheres [24], snow-like microcrystalline [26], nanoplate [27], nanosheet [28], and nanoflake [29]. However, the absorption range of this pure BWO nanosheet are limited over shorter wavelength > 450 nm in the solar spectrum [22,25]. The interface of semiconductor, particularly, 1D hierarchical structure have gained more attention as it improves the properties and applied in various field including medicine, electronics, biology, optics, and energy conversion systems [26,27,30,31]. A significant effort has been devoted to assemble 1D/2D layered structures such as nanosheets/nanorods [32], and nanowires/nanorods [33]. Among the ferrite family, cobalt ferrite (CoFe2O4) has inverse spinel structure with excellent magnetic properties [34]. CoFe2O4, p-type semiconductor possesses unique properties such as large magneto strictive coefficient, high coercivity, chemical stability, large magneto crystalline anisotropy and mechanical hardness [35,36].

To solve this issue, a p-n heterojunction constructed between p-type semiconductor CoFe2O4 and n-type semiconductor Bi2WO6. The internal electric field created effectively separates the photogenerated carriers for improved photocatalytic performance.

Transition metal tungstates (TMTs) are an important inorganic materials family with various potential applications over several decades [37–39]. MnWO4 (MWO), a p-type semiconductor with wide band gap (3.2 eV) and are of great research interest because of its unique electrochromic, multiferroic, and ionic properties [40]. However, MWO can only absorb UV light, resulting in low photocatalytic performance. Ternary photocatalysts comprising of BWO (n-type), CFO (p-type) and MWO (n-type) forming a n-p-n heterojunction with three metal oxides extends the response of visible light absorption range, and rapid separation of charge carriers with potential interfacial gradient improves the overall photocatalytic performance.

Quantum dots (QDs) are nano-sized materials showing attractive features including size/composition-dependent optical properties, tuneable absorption/emission spectrum and high absorption coefficient etc [41,42]. The most widely employed QDs includes CdS QDs [43], Graphene QDs [44], and carbon QDs [45]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with sp2 carbon clusters exists in amorphous or non-crystalline and also in fused form with sp3 carbons to form a diamond like structure [45]. The interfacing of 0D/2D nanomaterials provides more insights due to the ultra-small CQDs and well-matched interface to form a heterostructure [46]. The unique advantages of these nanostructures are the accessible area between the CQDs and planar interface, and the well-structured planar interface, and hence it increases the interfacial charge transfer. The ultrasmall nanostructures are in efficient contact with the contaminants which accelerate the charge transfer process.

Herein, we designed a CQDs embedded hierarchical MWO/CFO over BWO ultrathin nanosheets (UNs) with excellent visible light harvesting capability, and abundant reaction sites for photodegradation of CEFX. To obtain a better photodegradation performance against CEFX, the CQDs-MWO/CFO@UBWO nanohybrids were optimized by introduction of varying ratios of CFO, MWO, CQDs into the BWO UNs. The structural, morphological, optical, and textural properties were investigated in detail by various characterization techniques. The well-matched band structure of constructed CQDs-MWO/CFO@BWO nanohybrids exhibited improved performance after optimizing the different hybrid dosage. The influence of pH, and varying drug concentration were investigated. In addition, the possible photodegradation mechanism were proposed and explored via the results obtained from UV–vis DRS, ESR, and scavenging experiments. The photodegradation pathway of CEFX was proposed based on the intermediates detected by GC-MS/MS analysis. Finally, the toxicity of the end products was predicted using end product toxicity assessment against bacterial species.

Bismuth nitrate pentahydrate, cobalt chloride, ferric chloride hexahydrate, manganese chloride dihydrate, urea, citric acid monohydrate, sodium tungstate dihydrate, cetyl trimethylammonium bromide, sodium hydroxide, ammonia, Para-benzoquinone (p-BZQ), ethylene glycol, ethylene diamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA), tert-butyl alcohol (t-BuOH) were procured from Hi media private Ltd, India. Citric acid and oxalic acid were purchased from SD fine chem limited, India. All the chemicals procured were of analytical grade and used without further purification.

The ultrathin Bi2WO6 nanosheets (UBWO NSs) were prepared by facile hydrothermal process [47]. Firstly, Na2WO4 (400 mg) and CTAB (320 mg) were dissolved in 80 mL of deionized (DI) water. Then, Bi(NO3)3.5 H2O (970 mg) were dissolved into the above mixture under stirring. The mixture was continued to stir for 1 h. After stirring process, the suspension was transferred into 120 mL Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave and the temperature was maintained at 140 °C for 24 h. After gradual cool down to room temperature, the suspension was dried at 60 °C for 10 h.

The Quasi-CFO nanosheets were deposited on UBWO NSs using hydrothermal method with modifications from the previously reported literature [47]. The 0.10CFO:UBWO binanosheets (BT) prepared as follows: 0.5 g of UBWO NSs were dispersed in the solution containing 80 mL of CoCl2 (4.2 mM) and FeCl3.6 H2O (8.52 mM). The pH of the solution was adjusted to pH 11.0 using 4 M NaOH and the solution was stirred for 30 min. The concentration of precursors varied appropriately to get the desired X%CFO@UBWO NSs (X = 10 %, 25 % and 50 %), and were labelled as 0.10CFO:UBWO, 0.25CFO:UBWO, and 0.5CFO:UBWO NSs. The obtained solution was immediately transferred to Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave and subsequently, the temperature increased to 180 °C and maintained for 12 h. The CFO was prepared by the same method in absence of UBWO NSs.

For the synthesis of ternary MWO/CFO:UBWO nanohybrid, the prepared and optimized 0.10CFO:UBWO of 0.5 g was added to 40 mL of deionised water, and dispersed by sonication for 60 min. Then, 4 mM of MnCl2.2 H2O and 4 mM of Na2WO4.2 H2O were added to the dispersed solution. Subsequently, the pH of solution was adjusted to pH 9.0 using ammonia, and stirred for 45 min. The concentration of the precursor solution was varied appropriately to get the desired Y%MWO:CFO:UBWO (Y = 5 , 10 and 15 %), and labelled as 0.05MWO:CFO:UBWO, 0.10MWO:CFO:UBWO, and 0.15MWO:CFO:UBWO nanohybrid. The resultant solution was transferred to Teflon lined autoclave, sealed and maintained at 180 °C for 12 h. The obtained product was dried at 70 °C for 12 h.

To synthesize carbon quantum dots (CQDs), the urea and citric acid were dissolved in 50 mL DI water. Subsequently, the dissolved solution was transferred to hydrothermal, and heated to 180 °C for 5 h. After cooled down to room temperature, the product was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 30 min. Then, the supernatant was dried at 105 °C to obtain the CQDs. Then 0.5 g of MWO/CFO:UBWO nanohybrid was weighed, and dispersed in DI water for 30 min. Then, 2 g of CQDs was added to 20 mL of DI water, and homogenous CQDs solution of 1 mL, 2 mL and 5 mL were added drop wise to the dispersed MWO/CFO:UBWO to obtain 1 %CQDs @MWO/CFO:UBWO, 2 %CQDs@MWO/CFO:UBWO and 5 %CQDs@MWO/CFO:UBWO nanohybrid. Then, the solution was stirred for 30 min, and the solution was centrifuged to obtain CQDs@MWO/CFO:UBWO nanohybrid.

The surface morphology, and interface formed between the layered nanostructures of the prepared samples were investigated by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM). The obtained Bi2WO6 exhibits square-sheet like morphology with a porous rough surface ( Fig. 1a). The quasi-spherical nanosheet like morphology of CoFe2O4 (CFO) is observed in the supplementary material Fig. S1a. Tiny spherical balls observed reveal the morphology of MnWO4 (MWO) (Supplementary material Fig. S1b). The assembly of the tiny MWO spheres on the CFO/Bi2WO6 binanosheets (BT) are shown in Fig. 1b, and nevertheless, the presence of CQDs was hardly seen due to their small size. The HR-TEM analysis was carried out to better understand the morphological characteristics. The HR-TEM image provides more insights into arrangement of nanostructures and the distribution of CQDs. As shown in Fig. 1c, the ultrathin two-dimensional Bi2WO6 nanosheets (UBWO NSs) displayed square-sheet like structures with high transparency. The CFO nanosheets and MWO nanospheres well-embedded over the transparent UBWO NSs are observed as seen in the yellow-colored marking (Fig. 1c), and the small black dots observed are CQDs in Fig. 1d. The lattice interplanar distance of 0.314, 0.294, and 0.279 nm corresponds to the d-spacing of MWO (112), CFO (311), and UBWO (013), respectively (Fig. 1e). In addition, the elemental mapping image demonstrated the homogenous distribution of all the elements (Bi, W, Mn, Co, Fe, O, and C), and high purity with the absence of other foreign atoms (Supplementary material Fig. S2a). Similarly, the energy dispersive x-ray spectra (EDS) indicate the presence of elements in the nanohybrid (Supplementary material Fig. S2b). These results demonstrate the intimate fusion of CQDs over the CFO, MWO and UBWO, and the close contact established provides a good charge carrier transfer channel.

The crystalline nature and purity of prepared samples was investigated by X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis. Fig. 2a depicts the XRD pattern of the prepared samples. The peaks observed correspond to UBWO monoclinic phase with a lattice constant of a = 16.440 Å, b = 3.854 Å, c = 8.374 Å [48]. The 2Ɵ values at 28.5°, 33.18°, 47.32°, and 59.95° are assigned to (013), (211), (217) and (504) planes respectively. The highly organized diffraction peaks at 30.08°, 35.43°, 43.34°, 58.64°, and 63.64° corresponds to (220), (311), (400), (511), and (531) planes of cubic crystallographic phase of CFO, respectively [49]. The less intense peaks at 28.5°, 33.17°, 47.0° and 59.94° were observed in the nanohybrid diffraction pattern with lower intensity, confirming the assembly of CFO upon the UBWO [50]. Further, the decoration of MWO nanospheres upon the binary heterojunction were confirmed with the peaks observed at 36.07°, 40.06°, 53.1°, 62.96° and 74.33° corresponds to the (002), (102), (112), (311) and (232) planes of MWO [51]. The appearance of an additional peak at 34.1° can be attributed to the presence of MnO (JCPDS no. 75-0626) in the sample. This peak is highly likely to result from the interaction between Mn and O during the hydrothermal reaction [52]. The amount of CQDs loaded was small with high dispersion and thus, cannot be detected by XRD. Additionally, no other peaks and alteration in the crystalline phases were observed confirming the high purity and successful fabrication of the nanohybrid. The variation in peak intensities confirm that the CQDs, CFO, MWO were successfully deposited over UBWO. Further, the peak shift towards higher angles, and peak broadening of UBWO (211) facet indicates the influence of the deposited CFO, MWO on the UBWO, resulting in crystal lattice expansion. In addition, no changes were observed after CQDs deposition. This meant that the CQDs were loaded in small amount with high dispersion on MWO/CFO:UBWO nanohybrid (Fig. 2b). The Williamson-Hall (W-H) method utilized to evaluate the strain in the crystalline structure which directly affects the crystalline size and crystal structure of the material from the X-ray diffraction profiles of the nanohybrid. The crystalline strain and crystalline size are calculated using the W-H equation as follows (Eq. 1):
(1)
β c o s θ =
k λ
D
+ 4 C + ε + s i n θ
where β is the full-width half maximum (FWHM), D is the cyrstalline size, θ is the refelection angle, and ε is the strain value of crystal structure. The positive slope indicates the crystal lattice expansion after the nanohyrbid formation, and the calculated average crystalline size of the nanohyrbid is 67 nm (Fig. 2c).

To evaluate the chemical states and chemical composition of the as-synthesised nanohybrid, X-ray photoelectron (XPS) survey spectrum were employed and represented in Fig. 3. The elements detected based on the applied binding energy are Co 2p, Fe 2p3, Mn 2p, O1s, C1s, Bi 4 f and W 4 f species, which demonstrates the purity of the prepared nanohybrid (Fig. 3a). The XPS spectra of Bi 4 f are observed with binding energies of 164.31 and 162.9 eV, and 159.34 and 157.38 eV belong to 4 f4/2 and 4 f7/2 states of Bi2WO6 (Fig. 3b) [53–55]. The binding energies of 4 f doublet state at 33.8 eV (4 f7/2) and 35.9 eV (4 f5/2) indicates the W6+ state (Fig. 3c) [43,44]. After deconvolution, the curve fitting can be identified ≈ 30.3 % of W4+ (33.4 and 33.8 eV), and ≈ 41 % of W6+ (35.8 and 37.6 eV) (Fig. 3c). The binding energies at 529.0, 531.20 and 533.31 eV correspond to O1s states. The existence of lattice oxygen vacancy is confirmed by the peak at 531.20 eV [56]. The deconvoluted O1s peaks at 532.5 and 530.2 eV are assigned to the lattice and absorbed oxygen on the UBWO, respectively (Fig. 3d) [28]. The XPS spectra of Co 2p with two binding energies at 795.50 and 783.89 eV corresponds to Co 2p1/2 and Co 2p3/2 spin states (Fig. 3e) [49]. The XPS of Fe 2p as depicted in the Fig. 3 f, the Fe 2p states consists of two binding energy that is 2p1/2 and 2p3/2 are located at 724.06 and 710.17 eV. The Mn 2p spectra located at 651.53 and 640.74 eV with Mn3+ states (Fig. 3 g) [51]. A predominant peak of C1s found at 284.83 eV are assigned to CC, and the other peaks at 284.82, 286.82, and 288.85 eV belongs to C-O, CO and -COO bonds, respectively. No peaks Mn-C, Co-C, and Bi-C is found which further affirms that the CQDs do not exists as a dopant in the nanohybrid (Fig. 3 h)[55,57]. Therefore, the XPS results further affirmed the existence of the CFO, MWO and CQDs counterparts over the UBWO NSs, and successful formation of nanohybrid.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Light absorption, charge separation and transportation
ce:section.ce:para:
The band gap of the prepared samples and its light absorption properties were investigated by UV–vis DRS analysis [16]. Fig. 4a depicts the Kubelka-Munk (K-M) plot determined using UV–vis DRS analysis. The K-M equation represented below is used to calculate the band gap energy of the materials (Eq. 2):
(2)

where F(R) refers to K-M functions and R refers to reflectance co-efficient. The plot of [F(R)hv]1/2 vs hv were determined from diffuse reflectance spectroscopy, h is the planck’s constant, and v is the frequency [58]. The valence band potential (EVB) and conduction band potential (ECB) values were calculated by using the mulliken electronegativity theory by using the equation provided below (Eqs. 3 & 4):
(3)

where Eg, EVB, ECB, X, and E0 refers to band gap energy, valence band potential, conduction band potential, electronegativity, and free electron energy on H2 scale (−4.5 eV) respectively. The direct optical band gaps determined by K-M plot is 1.88, 2.67, 2.90 and 3.48 eV for MWO, UBWO, CFO, and nanohybrid, respectively. This improved light absorption in visible light can enhance the ability of the photocatalyst to absorb visible light and photoexcited e-/h+ pairs. However, the light absorption ability is just one parameter influencing the degradation process [59]. The other parameters include the efficient separation of photogenerated charge carriers and surface area, which play crucial role in determining the photocatalytic performance.

The bonding interaction between UBWO, CFO, and MWO were investigated by Raman spectra. Fig. 4b depicts the Raman spectra of the prepared samples. The five characteristics of UBWO are 797.22, 752.41, 725.98, 618.90, 310.66, and 292.74 cm−1 [56]. In detail, the Raman bands at 797.22, 752.98 and 725.98 cm−1 are assigned to W-O bands with symmetric and asymmetric modes. The Raman bands in the range from 110 to 420 cm−1 are assigned to the bending modes of WO6 octahedral. It has a simultaneous translation motion with Bi3+. The results are in correlation with the reported studies related with tungstate groups. The peaks at 1025 cm−1 corresponds to WO band which is only seen in UBWO photocatalyst [56]. The Raman peaks at 310.66 cm−1 are ascribed to the translational modes of Bi3+ and WO6 2- [60]. D or G bands corresponding to CQDs were not observed, and the reason may be as follows: (i) the low loaded amount of CQDs did not produce Raman bands with weak bands and (ii) the domains of carbons are converted to other form after interaction with the ternary complex structures, leading to the modification in the structural characteristics [61].

The specific surface area, pore radius and pore volume of the prepared samples were measured using Bruaner-Emmett-Teller (BET) analyzer. The surface area of the photocatalyst is an important factor to determine the photodegradation process. The surface area of UBWO, CFO, MWO and nanohybrid were 132.04, 137.40, 112.21 and 323.33 m2/g respectively. The specific surface area of nanohybrid was greater than the pure samples (Fig. 4c). These results confirm that the nanohybrid exhibited excellent photodegradation process when compared to individual materials. The surface area, pore volume and pore size are represented in Table 1 for all the prepared samples.

The PL intensity can directly determine the effective separation of the charge carriers. Generally, as the probability of recombination of charge carriers increases, the photoluminescence (PL) intensity tends to decrease. Conversely, the low PL intensity corresponds to the less probability of recombination of charge carriers [62]. As depicted in Fig. 4d, the PL intensity is much lower for the 5 %CQDs/MWO(0.25)/CFO(0.10):UBO compared with other counterparts. Thus, the reduced charge carrier’s recombination can effectively increase the participation of charge carriers in the photodegradation process.

In addition, the separation and transfer of photoinduced electron-hole pairs were investigated by EIS analysis. The Nyquist plots of UBWO, CFO, MWO and nanohybrid are depicted in Fig. 4e. Generally, the EIS Nyquist plot smaller arc radius corresponds to the greater rate of photogenerated charge carriers. However, the greater arc radius ascribed to the resistance of transfer of charge carriers. For the prepared samples, the size of the arc radius decreases as follows: nanohybrid>UBWO>CFO>MWO. Thus, the enhanced separation of charge carriers is confirmed from the PL and EIS analysis.

The evaluation of photodegradation performance of the prepared samples was performed under visible light towards CEFX. Here, 20 mg/L CEFX concentration were used to investigate the performance with 15 mg/L concentration of the prepared samples at pH 5. The photodegradation performance of the prepared samples under visible light irradiation towards the degradation of CEFX is depicted in Fig. 5b and supplementary material Fig. S2a. The concentration of CFO was optimized to understand the effect and improve the photocatalytic performance. The higher degradation was exhibited by 0.1CFO over UBWO NSs (79.3 % at 180 min) compared with other concentrations (0.25CFO and 0.50CFO) on UBWO and thus the UBWO/10 %CFO was selected for further studies. The UBWO and CFO form n-p heterojunction. The performance of the binary optimized UBWO/0.10CFO photocatalyst were improved by interfacing it with a visible light active n-type MWO to form n-p-n heterojunction. Further, the effect on loading different MWO concentrations (0.05, 0.10 and 0.15) was found to improve the performance of the photocatalyst. The improved photocatalytic efficiency is shown by 0.15MWO (83.66 %) over the binary heterojunction (Supplementary material Fig. S5a). Further, the degradation rates (k value) were 4.69, 1.78, and 1.36 times greater than 0.05MWO, 0.15MWO, and bare MWO respectively [63]. The MWO coupled with UBWO/0.1CFO greatly influenced the photocatalytic performance, and the reasons for the improved performance are explained as follows: (1) The deposition of CFO tune the band gap of the binary heterojunction, improving the visible light harvesting capability, thus enhancing the photocatalytic performance of the material as indicated by the UV–vis DRS results [64], (2) The formation of n-p-n heterojunction between UBWO, CFO and MWO creates a built-in electric field favouring the charge transfer leading to spatial separation of photoexcited charge carriers [65]. The greater loading of MWO on the binary heterojunction enhances the light absorption of UBWO/0.1CFO, increasing the photocatalytic activity. Thus, the optimized 0.10CFO and 0.25MWO loaded UBWO NSs exhibited excellent photocatalytic degradation of CEFX.

Different CQDs mass ratios of 1, 2 & 5 % were incorporated upon the optimized ternary heterojunction to understand the effect on photocatalytic performance. As depicted Supplementary material Fig. S3a, the rate of degradation with different CQDs of 1, 2 and 5 % were 0.0026, 0.0036, and 0.0160 min−1 respectively. It is evident that the increase in CQDs mass ratios increased the photodegradation of CEFX. The degradation efficiency of 1 %CQDs, 2 %CQDs and 5 %CQDs were 68.13, 62.53 and 97.06 % respectively. The improved efficiency attributed to the increase in active sites, and the enhanced absorption of visible light to produce more photogenerated electron and hole pairs [65–67]. The overall kinetic study results of all prepared samples are listed in Supplementary material Table S1. Further, the UV–vis absorption spectral change of the optimized 5 %CQDs decorated ternary nanohybrid under visible light irradiation were performed and are depicted in Fig. 5a. The intensity of the UV–vis absorption spectra at λmax = 286 nm decreased with increase in the illumination time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of different parameters – pH, CEFX and NCs dosage
ce:section.ce:para: The environmental factors such as pH can significantly influence the photodegradation process. To investigate the pH influence, the pH of the reaction mixture (initial pH 5) was adjusted to obtain a range from pH 4.0 to pH 8.0. Fig. 5c represents the plot of C/C0 vs time (min) at different pH range. The complete degradation of CEFX was observed at pH 7.0 within 160 min. The increase in the pH to above or below pH 7.0 hinders the photodegradation efficiency. The rate of degradation at pH 7.0 was 1.64, 1.03, 1.19 and 1.39 times greater than the degradation efficiency at pH 4.0, pH 5.0, pH 6.0, and pH 8.0, respectively (Figs. 5d and 5e). The reason for the improved performance at neutral pH is due to the electrostatic interaction of the positively charged nanohybrid with the negatively charged CEFX. Thus, it can be concluded that the prepared nanohybrid can be applied for practical applications. The results of the kinetic study for photodegradation of CEFX at different pH are listed in Supplementary material Table S2.

The rate of photodegradation also depends on the catalyst concentration. The impact of the catalyst concentration was investigated over a range from 5 to 25 mg/L. Supplementary material Fig. 3b1-b2 depicts the results of impact of photocatalyst concentration where the 15 mg/L catalyst concentration showed higher degradation which was 97.06 % at 200 min (at pH 5). The rate of degradation was predominantly affected with increase or decrease in the catalyst concentration. The diminished performance with the increase in catalyst concentration is due to the light screening effect. In detail, as the concentration of catalyst reaches an optimal value where the further increase retards the reaction process due to excess concentration of catalyst tends to block the light penetration. The results of the kinetic study for photodegradation of CEFX at different dosages are listed in Supplementary material Table S3. Therefore, the study suggests that optimal catalyst concentration favouring the photodegradation process was 15 mg/L.

The photodegradation processes that occur at the catalyst’s surface are influenced by the number of CEFX molecules. The efficiency of the photodegradation process under varying catalyst dosage from 10 to 50 mg/L was investigated. The results of the rate of photodegradation at varying catalyst dosage are depicted in Supplementary material Fig. 3C1-C2 . The rate of degradation was relatively very high at 10 mg/L (99.31 %) whereas the further increase reduces the photodegradation efficiency. Supplementary material Table S3 lists the k value and degradation (%) at various CEFX dosages. This can be attributed to the fact that excessive amounts of CEFX molecules block the active sites by covering the catalyst surfaces. Further, the study implies that the degradation performance was influenced greatly by CEFX dosage.

To demonstrate the feasibility of the photocatalyst for practical applications, the recycling experiments were carried out, and the results are depicted in Supplementary material Fig. 4a. The degradation efficiencies were still maintained more than 96 % even after six consecutive photocatalytic reactions. Moreover, the structural stability of the nanohybrid analysis after the recycling experiments was investigated by XRD and XPS analysis. As depicted in Supplementary material Fig. 4b, the characteristics diffraction peaks were consistent with the fresh composite, and no extra peaks could be observed. Besides, XPS of the reused catalyst were examined and the XPS survey scan demonstrated the presence of all elements. The chemical shift was not observed indicating the high stability of the nanohybrid even after six cycle and insignificant leaching of chemicals in the nanohybrid (Fig. 3a-g). The XRD and XPS results revealed that nanohybrid possesses high chemical stability. In addition, the leaching of element from the catalyst was studied by atomic absorption spectrophotometer analysis (AAS) after photocatalysis process and the study indicated that significant amount of ion leaching was not observed, which further confirmed the stability of the particles.

The mechanism of photodegradation was mainly focused on band structure and radical involved during the pollutant’s degradation [68]. To identify the free radicals participating in photocatalytic mechanism, the radical scavenging experiments were performed [69]. The addition of t-BuOH and p-BZQ diminished the photodegradation rate of the nanohybrid from 97.8 % to 31 % and 25 %, respectively as depicted in Supplementary material Fig. 4c. Therefore, the O2 •- and •OH played crucial role in the photodegradation process. Moreover, the reactive oxidation species participated was further verified by ESR analysis. The DMPO-OH and DMPO-O2- signals are not observed in dark for all the photocatalysts. Fig. 4c represents the ESR spectra of the prepared samples. Under light irradiation, the ESR signals of the binary heterojunctions were greater intensity than nanohybrid. Thus, the results reveal the involvement of more radicals during the photodegradation process, and the results are consistent with the scavenging experiments.

The band structure studied from UV–vis DRS results, efficient photocatalytic performance revealed by the photocatalytic experiments, effective charge separation and transfer implied from PL and EIS results provided a path to propose a plausible mechanism of photocatalytic performance. Based on the band gaps obtained from the UV–vis DRS results, the ECB and EVB of UBWO, MWO, and CFO were calculated to be 0.24, 0.37, − 0.12 eV and 3.14, 2.25, 3.35 eV vs standard hydrogen potential, respectively. The energy band structure before the contact was depicted in Fig. 6 (left).

Upon visible light irradiation, the photogenerated charge carriers are generated and the photoexcited electron and holes are transferred to the [WO4]2- middle layer and [BiO]+ surface layers, respectively. The oxygen vacancy generated in the UBWO cause a remarkable electronic state distortion facilitating the dissociation of excitons and then improves the concentration of charge carrier. Thus, the electrons can travel to the [WO4]2- layer and coordinates with the unsaturated W sites, and the holes travel to [BiO]+ layer to the O atom bonded to nearby W atoms. After contact establishment between the three materials, the electron in the CB of p-type CFO transferred to CB of n-type UBWO and n-type MWO until the fermi level reaches an equilibrium state. This phenomenon leads to band bending of the semiconductors and an internal electric field is created at the contact interface. Under the built-in electric field, photogenerated electrons are transferred easily from the CB of CFO to CB of UBWO and MWO. Subsequently, the holes present in the CB of UBWO and MWO moves towards the p-type CFO. In this way, the electron and holes are effectively separated. The CQDs decorated over the MWO/CFO:UBWO nanohybrid possess unique upconversion property owing to its quantum confinement effect. There are two possible energy transfers that can happen between CQDs and MWO/CFO:UBWO nanohybrid. Here, the lower energetic photons are absorbed by CQDs where the electron and hole pairs formed recombine and emit a high energy photons. These high energy photons aids in electrons excitation in the CFO, MWO and UBWO. Secondly, the excited electrons move from the excited state to the CB of MWO. The electrons attack the molecular oxygen to produce superoxide radical and holes produce hydroxy radical. To sum up, the hydroxyl radical and superoxide radical acted as main reactive oxygen species for the degradation of CEFX and this proposed mechanism verified the crucial role of the n-p-n heterojunction and the CQDs for great improvement in the photocatalytic performance.

To understand the CEFX degradation process in detail, the intermediates formed during the process were identified by GC-MS/MS analysis. The photodegradation pathway is displayed in Fig. 7. There are seven intermediates detected using the GC-MS/MS. The identified intermediates are with the m/z value of 365.11, 343.12, 227.18, 163.06, 118.09, 83.06 and 60.05 named as in order from C1 to C9, respectively. As depicted in Fig. 7, the thiazole group was loosed with the initial decomposition process, leading to the formation of C1. Then, the C1 was degraded to C2 with the decomposition of β-lactam to smaller structure. The degradation was further prolonged to form C3 and C4. In the second degradation pathway, the majority of CEFX was degraded to C5 with the destruction in the thiazole side and β-lactam. The opening of β-lactam leads to the formation of C5 and further degraded to C6, C7, C8 and C9. The prolonged oxidation/reactions led to the molecule’s decomposition to CO2, NO3 -, SO4 2- and H2O.

The acute and chronic toxicity of the intermediates and CEFX were predicted towards aquatic organisms (Fish, green algae, daphnid) by ECOSAR software. According to the classsification of global harmonized system, the toxicity level were ranged from very toxic, toxic, harmful and non-harmful. The toxicity of CEFX, and the intermediates identified from GC-MS/MS analysis were placed according to the toxicity level as Fig. 8. In both acute and chronic toxicity, the parent coumpound are ranged in not harmful category towards green algae, daphnid and fish. The C1 and C2 intermediates are categorized under not harmful towards fish. The intermediates, C3 and C4 are categorized as very toxic. Although, the intermediates are predicted to be toxic wherein the C6, C7 and C8 final end products are in the not harmful category. Thus, the end products are not harmful as affirmed by the eco-toxicity prediction analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: End product toxicity assessment against bacterial species
ce:section.ce:para: The evaluation of potential toxicity of the end-products is crucial to understand the effectiveness of the proposed study towards the environment. The toxicity of end products against bacterial was determined using bacterial growth inhibition assay. The toxicity of the CEFX before photodegradation and after the photodegradation was evaluated based on the rate of inhibition of bacterial species (E. coli and S. epidermis). The toxicity (%) before the photodegradation were 94.30 %, and 82.38 % towards E. coli and S. epidermis respectively whereas the toxicity (%) completely decreased after the photodegradation to 11.31 % and 5.59 % against E. coli and S. epidermis respectively (Supplementary material Fig. S6d). The complete mineralization of the CEFX after photodegradation process was evident from the end product toxic assessment.

In this work, a series of CQDs embedded ultrathin BWO nanosheets hierarchically layered with Quasi sheet like CFO and MWO nanospheres (CQDs@MWO/CFO:UBO nanohybrid) were successfully fabricated and assembled via facile hydrothermal method. An excellent photocatalytic performance was achieved by 5 %CQDs loaded CFO/MWO@BWO UNs towards the degradation of CEFX (97.06 % 200 min) under visible light irradiation after optimizing the different CFO, and MWO concentrations. The morphological and structural characteristics investigated by SEM and HR-TEM image evident the successful assembly of the nanohybrid. The extraordinary performance of the optimized 5 %CQDs on ternary heterojunction are attributed to the large surface area, narrow band gap and internal electric field at contact interface promoting more charge carriers. The trapping experiments affirmed the involvement of superoxide and hydroxyl radicals. It was found that the nanohybrid retained its degradation efficiency after being used for six times. Furthermore, the end products were less toxic as affirmed from end product toxicity assessment. This work provides more insights for the development of new approaches for optimization of heterojunction to improve the efficiency of degradation of toxic pollutants.
